title: Hyperkalemia

sections.title: Definition & Severity
sections.content: Hyperkalemia is serum K+ >5.0 mEq/L. It's a potentially lethal electrolyte disorder that requires rapid recognition and treatment.
keyPoints: Critical: >7.0 mEq/L or ANY ECG changes (emergency)
First rule out pseudohyperkalemia (hemolysis, prolonged tourniquet, high WBC/platelets)

sections.title: ECG Changes - The Progression
sections.content: ECG changes progress predictably with rising potassium, though individual variation exists.
keyPoints: 5.5-6.5: Peaked T waves (tall, narrow, symmetric - earliest sign)
6.5-7.5: Prolonged PR interval, flattened P waves
8.0-9.0: Loss of P waves, sine wave pattern
CRITICAL: ECG changes indicate cardiac membrane instability - treat as emergency regardless of K+ level
Some patients have ECG changes at 6.0; others tolerate 7.5 - treat based on ECG, not just number

sections.title: Causes
sections.content: Hyperkalemia results from increased intake, transcellular shift, or decreased excretion.

subsections.name: Increased Intake (rarely sole cause)
items: Dietary (unusual unless renal impairment present)
IV potassium overdose
Blood transfusion (stored blood has high K+)
Salt substitutes (KCl)

subsections.name: Transcellular Shift (K+ moves OUT of cells)
items: Acidosis (H+ enters cells, K+ exits - 0.6 mEq/L rise per 0.1 pH drop)
Insulin deficiency/hyperglycemia
Beta-blockers (prevent cellular K+ uptake)
Cell lysis: rhabdomyolysis, tumor lysis, hemolysis, burns
Digoxin toxicity (inhibits Na-K-ATPase)
Succinylcholine (in burns, crush, paralysis)
Hyperkalemic periodic paralysis

subsections.name: Decreased Excretion (most common)
items: Acute or chronic kidney disease (GFR <20 = high risk)
Hypoaldosteronism: Addison's, hyporeninemic hypoaldosteronism (diabetes)
Medications: ACEi, ARBs, K+-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs, trimethoprim, heparin
Type 4 RTA

sections.title: Emergency Treatment Algorithm
sections.content: Treatment has three phases: stabilize the heart, shift K+ into cells, and remove K+ from body.

subsections.name: 1. STABILIZE THE HEART (works in 1-3 minutes)
items: Calcium gluconate 1-2g IV over 2-5 min (or calcium chloride via central line)
Does NOT lower K+ - just stabilizes cardiac membrane
Duration: 30-60 minutes - may need to repeat
Give if ANY ECG changes or K+ >6.5

subsections.name: 2. SHIFT K+ INTO CELLS (works in 15-30 minutes)
items: Insulin 10 units regular IV + D50 (25-50g glucose) - MOST EFFECTIVE
Lowers K+ by 0.5-1.0 mEq/L for 4-6 hours
Albuterol 10-20mg nebulized (high dose) - lowers K+ 0.5-1.0 mEq/L
Can combine insulin + albuterol for additive effect
Sodium bicarbonate: minimal acute effect, reserve for severe acidosis
MONITOR GLUCOSE: give with D50, recheck in 1 hour (hypoglycemia risk 10-20%)

subsections.name: 3. REMOVE K+ FROM BODY (definitive but slow)
items: Loop diuretics (furosemide 40-80mg IV) - if kidney function preserved
Kayexalate (sodium polystyrene sulfonate): slow onset (hours), GI side effects, risk of bowel necrosis
Patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate: newer binders, better tolerated
HEMODIALYSIS: definitive treatment for severe hyperkalemia, especially with renal failure

sections.title: Special Situations
sections.content: Certain clinical scenarios require modified approaches.
keyPoints: DIGOXIN TOXICITY: Avoid calcium (increases dig toxicity) - use digibind
DKA: K+ may be high initially but total body K+ is LOW; as you treat with insulin, K+ will drop precipitously - add K+ to fluids when <5.3
Massive transfusion: anticipate hyperkalemia from stored blood
Crush injury: anticipate massive K+ release; consider early dialysis
Cardiac arrest from hyperK: prolonged resuscitation may succeed if K+ lowered

keyTakeaways: ECG changes trump K+ level - treat based on cardiac effects
Three-step treatment: Stabilize heart (calcium) → Shift K+ (insulin/albuterol) → Remove K+ (dialysis/diuretics/binders)
Calcium stabilizes heart but doesn't lower K+ - it's a bridge
Insulin is most effective K+-lowering agent; always give with glucose
Monitor for hypoglycemia after insulin (10-20% risk)
Definitive removal requires dialysis if kidneys aren't working
In DKA, K+ will plummet as you treat - prepare to replace it

reviewQuestions.question: A patient with CKD has K+ 7.2 and peaked T waves on ECG. What is the FIRST treatment?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Calcium gluconate is FIRST because it immediately stabilizes the cardiac membrane (works in 1-3 min). It doesn't lower K+ but prevents arrhythmia while you give insulin/albuterol to shift K+ and arrange dialysis for removal.

reviewQuestions.question: After giving insulin 10U IV with D50 for hyperkalemia, what must you monitor?
reviewQuestions.explanation: Hypoglycemia occurs in 10-20% of patients after insulin for hyperkalemia, even with D50. Recheck glucose at 1 hour and give additional dextrose if needed. The insulin effect on K+ lasts 4-6 hours.

reviewQuestions.question: A patient in DKA has K+ 6.2 on initial labs. As you start insulin, what should you anticipate?
options: K+ will drop rapidly - prepare to replace
You should hold insulin due to hyperK
reviewQuestions.explanation: In DKA, total body K+ is DEPLETED (lost in urine), but serum K+ is high due to shift out of cells (insulin deficiency + acidosis). As you correct with insulin, K+ shifts back INTO cells and can drop dangerously. Add K+ to fluids when <5.3.

references: Sterns RH. Treatment of hyperkalemia. UpToDate 2024
Palmer BF. Regulation of Potassium Homeostasis. CJASN 2015
Kovesdy CP. Management of hyperkalaemia in CKD. Nat Rev Nephrol 2014
